dialog.confirm_clear_message: Are you sure? {count} results will be deleted.
overlay.title: Analyzing files...
popup.verified.2: Quality is low but no deception
popup.explanation.1: This file claims to be a {fmt} at {declared}, but spectral analysis shows the actual content cuts off at {cutoff}, which corresponds to {real} audio. This indicates the file was converted (transcoded) from a very low quality source, likely a YouTube or streaming rip.
popup.explanation.2: This file is a {fmt} at {declared}. Analysis confirms the quality matches what's declared, with a frequency cutoff at {cutoff}. While not a transcode, the quality is low: high-frequency detail is lost, which may be noticeable in mixes and on quality sound systems.
popup.explanation.3: This file presents itself as {fmt} at {declared}, but analysis shows a cutoff at {cutoff}, indicating an actual quality of approximately {real}. The file was converted from a medium quality source. It's functional for casual listening, but you'll lose treble detail and brightness in professional mixes.
popup.explanation.4: This file is a {fmt} at {declared}. Analysis confirms a frequency cutoff at {cutoff}, which matches the declared bitrate. Quality is acceptable for general listening, but limited for professional use in DJ sets or production.
popup.explanation.5: Verified {fmt} file at {declared}. Spectral content extends to {cutoff}, confirming this is a genuinely high quality file. The declared bitrate matches the detected quality. Suitable for professional use in DJ sets and production.
popup.explanation.6: This file is a {fmt} at {declared}, a lossless format that should contain frequencies up to 22 kHz. However, content cuts off at {cutoff}, suggesting the original file may have been an MP3 at ~{real} that was converted to {fmt}. Quality is good but not true lossless.
popup.explanation.7: {fmt} file at {declared} with full spectral content exceeding {cutoff}. This is lossless (uncompressed) audio, the highest quality available. No compression or transcode detected. Perfect quality for any professional use.
popup.explanation.8: {fmt} file at {declared} with spectral content extending to {cutoff}. This is the maximum quality possible for the MP3 format. Analysis confirms this is a genuinely high quality file with no transcode.
popup.explanation.9: {fmt} file with declared bitrate of {declared}. However, spectral content extends to {cutoff}, placing its actual quality around {real}. While it doesn't reach the maximum quality of a native {declared}, the file retains a wide frequency range and is suitable for professional use in DJ sets and production.
popup.explanation.10: {fmt} file with declared bitrate of {declared}. Spectral content extends to {cutoff}, very close to the 20 kHz limit of a native {declared}. The difference is minimal and practically imperceptible on a professional sound system. This is a good quality file.
editor.unsupported_format: This format does not support metadata editing.
editor.field_compilation_text: This is a compilation by various artists
